title: SISMID: Human-AI Teaming
text:
Please download the software specified on the Overview page as soon as possible. Because most of the practicum runs through GitHub, please create a GitHub account before the Day One practicum. If you plan to work in a browser-based GitHub Codespace rather than locally, please make sure your account is set up and ready ahead of time; if you plan to work locally, please install VS Code and Git as well. Please reach out if you need any help.
The course materials repository has both video and written tutorials available for getting set up with GitHub, VS Code, and the AI coding assistant, and we highly recommend that both be viewed. We will also walk through the setup and Activity 1 (Data Cleaning) together during the lab on Day One of the module.
The Additional Resources page contains tutorials on how to create a GitHub account and launch a Codespace, set up VS Code and the AI coding assistant, download both R and RStudio, and download packages in R.

title: SISMID: Human-AI Teaming
text:
Module Summary
This module introduces key concepts in infectious disease modeling alongside core principles of human–AI teaming. Students will begin with a guided walkthrough of AI prompting using an example dataset, demonstrating how to build a full forecasting pipeline step-by-step. This demo is followed by hands-on labs where students apply the same prompting strategies to a more comprehensive dataset using AI tools and basic R. Sessions integrate brief lectures with practical exercises, emphasizing how humans guide, validate, and refine AI-generated analyses—including data cleaning, visualization, model building, and forecasting.

title: SISMID: Human-AI Teaming
text:
Prerequisites
This module assumes a basic working knowledge of the R programming language, including running scripts, installing packages, and working with data in the R environment. Students should be comfortable navigating files and folders on their computer and have familiarity with common coding workflows. Prior experience with version control systems is not required but will be beneficial.

Required Software
Software: R, RStudio, Visual Studio Code (VS Code), Git, GitHub Account, GitHub Copilot
Required Setup:

Install the latest versions of R and RStudio.
Install Visual Studio Code and the R extension.
Install Git and configure it for use with GitHub.
Create a GitHub account if you do not already have one.
Obtain access to GitHub Copilot.

Students planning to use GitHub Copilot should complete the registration process at least 72 hours before the module to allow sufficient time for account verification and activation. Students with a valid .edu email address are encouraged to apply for the GitHub Education Program, which includes access to GitHub Copilot benefits. Additional information can be found through GitHub Education.
Students without GitHub Education access may use the free version of GitHub Copilot, which includes usage limitations. The GitHub Copilot Pro plan is recommended for this module and includes a free trial period for eligible users. Additional details regarding software installation and configuration will be provided in the module setup materials.

title: SISMID: Human-AI Teaming
text:
Module Content

Overview of infectious disease forecasting and its role in public health decision-making
Foundations of AI and human-AI teaming, including when to use which AI tools for a given task
Introduction to GitHub and VS Code for reproducible, collaborative forecasting workflows
Data cleaning and preparation of surveillance data for forecasting
Effective prompting for AI-assisted coding and analysis
Using rules.md files to guide and constrain AI collaborators
Hands-on forecasting labs: fitting models and generating forecasts
Overview of forecast model evaluation
Assessing forecast calibration and performance in practice
AI and human-AI teaming evaluation, with iterative model refinement using AI
Independent free-coding, questions, and wrap-up

title: SISMID: Human-AI Teaming
text:
Instructors

Lior Rennert, PhD
Dr. Lior Rennert is Associate Dean for Health Sciences in the College of Behavioral, Social, and Health Sciences, Associate Professor of Biostatistics in the Department of Public Health Sciences, and Founding Director of the Clemson Center for Public Health Modeling and Response. He received his PhD degree in Biostatistics from the University of Pennsylvania’s Pearlman School of Medicine, MS degree in Statistics from the University of Chicago, and BS in Mathematics from The Pennsylvania State University. During the COVID-19 pandemic, Dr. Rennert led Clemson’s Public Health Strategy team to provide data analytics and help guide university policy. His current work focuses on the development of data-driven approaches to guide health-related decision making. His research has led to over 90 high-impact publications in the top scientific, medical, and health journals. He has procured approximately $30 million in funding as principal investigator from the National Institutes of Health and Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. His projects aim to develop a statewide network for real-time outbreak detection, forecasting, and coordinating emergency health response. In these efforts, Dr. Rennert has helped build statewide and national collaborations between state health departments, health systems, community partners, and academic institutions in order to integrate Center research into practice and ultimately improve health outcomes across the state of South Carolina and the nation.
Email: liorr@clemson.edu


Nathan McNeese, PhD
Dr. Nathan J. McNeese is the Associate Vice President for Technology & Innovation, the McQueen Quattlebaum Endowed Professor of Human-Centered Computing, the Founding Director of the Clemson University Center for Human-AI Interaction, Collaboration, & Teaming, and the Founding Director of the Team Research and Analytics in Computational Environments (TRACE) Research Group at Clemson University. In his AVP role, Dr. McNeese leads university-wide initiatives in artificial intelligence (AI) strategy, research, and innovation. He previously held the CECAS Dean’s Professorship at Clemson prior to his current AVP and endowed appointments. He received a PhD in Information Sciences & Technology from The Pennsylvania State University. A pioneer in human-AI collaboration and teaming, Dr. McNeese has been instrumental in advancing human-centered approaches to AI research and system development. He has been a principal investigator or co-principal investigator for more than 30 research grants and awards, generating over $40 million in funding. He is a recipient of the prestigious NSF CAREER Award. His research work is funded by the U.S. National Science Foundation, Air Force Office of Scientific Research, Office of Naval Research, Army Research Office, Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality, Department of Education, and several other industry and state agencies. He currently serves on multiple international/societal programs and technical committees, in addition to multiple editorial boards. He has worked with the United Nations, the North Atlantic Trade Organization (NATO), and multiple other countries and international organizations to provide guidance on human-centered AI. He is a National Academy of Science, Engineering, and Medicine (NASEM) board member on the Board of Human Systems Integration (BOHSI), a previous member of multiple National Academy of Sciences initiatives and consensus studies, and a previous member of the Army Research Lab HRED Technical Advisory Board. He is the recipient of the Clemson University Researcher of the Year, the Clemson University Research, Scholarship and Artistic Achievement Award, the HFES William C. Howell Young Investigator Award, and The Pennsylvania State University College of Information Sciences & Technology Overall Outstanding Alumni Award among additional significant honors. His research has received multiple best paper awards/nominations (9 total) and has been published in top peer-reviewed human-computer interaction and human factors venues over 200 times.
Email: mcneese@clemson.edu


TA: Amanda Bleichrodt, PhD
Dr. Amanda Bleichrodt is a postdoctoral fellow in the Center for Public Health Modeling and Response, specializing in infectious disease modeling and forecasting. She received her Master of Public Health from The Ohio State University, and a doctorate from Georgia State University in population health Sciences with a concentration in epidemiology. Her research interests include using AI for evaluating, refining and applying various statistical and mathematical modeling toolkits for forecasting disease outbreaks and developing user-friendly forecasting tools.
Email: ambleic@clemson.edu

title: SISMID: Human-AI Teaming
text:
Welcome to Day 1 of the course. Over the next three days (July 22 to 24) you will work as a human-AI team to build a national influenza hospitalization forecasting pipeline from real CDC data, all the way to a FluSight-ready model.
Today is about setup and your first pass through the workflow: get GitHub and VS Code working, learn how we team with an AI assistant, then clean the data and produce your first epicurve.
The tools are just tools. The real work is the modeling, and you stay the expert making the decisions.

title: SISMID: Human-AI Teaming
text:
Welcome to Day 1 of the course. Over the next three days (July 22 to 24) you will work as a human-AI team to build a national influenza hospitalization forecasting pipeline from real CDC data, all the way to a FluSight-ready model.
Today is about setup and your first pass through the workflow: get GitHub and VS Code working, learn how we team with an AI assistant, then clean the data and produce your first epicurve.
The tools are just tools. The real work is the modeling, and you stay the expert making the decisions.

title: SISMID: Human-AI Teaming
text:
What today covers
By the end of today you will be able to:

Fork the course repository and clone it into VS Code
Install the R extension and set up GitHub Copilot as your AI assistant
Explain how rules.md (written by you) and agents.md (generated by AI) fit together
Run the Human-AI Iteration Loop end to end on the data cleaning phase
Commit your results to your fork and open a pull request to the class repository

title: Day 1: Getting Started
text:
Before we start: setup check
You have two ways to work in this course. Pick whichever gets you to a working setup fastest.






NoteOption A: Your own machine



If your local R, Quarto, and editor are already working, use them. Confirm you can open the course repo, render a .qmd file, and load the modeling packages.








TipOption B: Cloud fallback (Codespaces)



If your local setup gives you trouble, open the course repo in a GitHub Codespace. It comes preloaded with the R modeling stack and Quarto, so you can skip straight to the modeling. This runs on your own free Codespaces hours.



Sign in to an AI assistant
Inside the Codespace you can choose one assistant to sign into:

Claude Code
GitHub Copilot
OpenAI Codex

Pick one and confirm it responds inside your editor before the first activity. You only need one.

title: Day 1: Getting Started
section: How we work: AI writes code, modeler decides
text:
How we work: AI writes code, modeler decides
The pattern for every activity is the same. You describe what you want in plain language, the assistant drafts the code, and you decide whether it is right.

The assistant is fast at boilerplate, syntax, and plumbing
You own the modeling choices: what to fit, what to trust, what to change
A rules.md file in each repo tells the assistant the constraints for that task, so its drafts stay inside the design you have chosen

The habit to build today: read every draft before you run it, and be ready to say why you accepted or rejected it.






Important



The assistant is a teammate, not an oracle. If a forecast looks wrong, it is your job to notice, not the assistant’s.

title: Day 1: Getting Started
text:
Today’s activity

Activity 3: GAM-based forecasting pipeline
You will build a forecasting pipeline around a generalized additive model using mgcv. Work through it with your AI assistant, but make the modeling calls yourself.

Start from the activity repo and read its rules.md
Fit the GAM and inspect the smooth terms
Produce forecasts and sanity-check them before you submit

Answer keys are available after you have made a genuine attempt. Try it first.
